text: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2016 recommends initial fluid resuscitation with at least 30 mL/kg of intravenous crystalloid within the first 3 hours for patients with sepsis-induced hypoperfusion. Dynamic rather than static variables should guide further fluid administration.

Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021 supports earlier initiation of vasopressors, potentially before completion of fluid resuscitation, in septic shock to maintain MAP >= 65 mmHg. Norepinephrine is first-line; vasopressin can be added to raise MAP or decrease norepinephrine dose.

VANISH trial showed early vasopressin in septic shock did not reduce kidney failure-free days compared with norepinephrine. Vasopressin remains an adjunct to reduce norepinephrine doses in refractory shock.
ANDROMEDA-SHOCK trial demonstrated peripheral perfusion-targeted resuscitation may be non-inferior to lactate-targeted resuscitation for septic shock and may avoid over-resuscitation.

Sepsis-3 defines sepsis as life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection. Septic shock requires vasopressor to maintain MAP >= 65 mmHg and serum lactate > 2 mmol/L despite adequate volume resuscitation.

qSOFA score (respiratory rate >= 22, altered mental status, systolic BP <= 100) identifies patients at risk of poor outcomes from infection outside the ICU. Lactate and early antibiotics within one hour of recognition reduce mortality.

Each hour of delay in antibiotic administration in septic shock is associated with approximately 7% increased mortality. Empiric broad-spectrum coverage is recommended within one hour of sepsis recognition.
source: Kumar A et al. Crit Care Med 2006

text: Norepinephrine is the recommended first-line vasopressor for septic shock. Phenylephrine is reserved for tachyarrhythmia-limited use. Dopamine is no longer recommended as first-line due to arrhythmic side effects.
source: De Backer D et al. NEJM 2010

text: Low-dose hydrocortisone (200mg/day) is suggested for septic shock not responding to fluid resuscitation and vasopressor therapy. The ADRENAL and APROCCHSS trials showed modest mortality benefit in severe refractory septic shock.
source: Annane D et al. NEJM 2018; Venkatesh B et al. NEJM 2018

text: The SSC Hour-1 bundle requires measuring lactate, obtaining blood cultures, administering broad-spectrum antibiotics, beginning rapid fluid resuscitation, and applying vasopressors for hypotension — all initiated within one hour of sepsis recognition.
source: Levy MM et al. Crit Care Med 2018

text: The SMART trial demonstrated that balanced crystalloids (lactated Ringer's) reduced the composite of death, dialysis, and persistent renal dysfunction compared to normal saline in critically ill adults.
The ALBIOS trial found that albumin added to crystalloid resuscitation in severe sepsis did not improve 90-day survival. Albumin may be considered when large volumes of crystalloid are needed.
Procalcitonin-guided antibiotic de-escalation in sepsis reduces antibiotic duration without increasing mortality. Serial procalcitonin can inform decisions about antibiotic discontinuation.
source: de Jong E et al. Lancet Infect Dis 2016

text: The SEPSISPAM trial showed that targeting a higher MAP of 80-85 mmHg did not reduce mortality compared to the standard 65-70 mmHg target in septic shock, but higher targets increased atrial fibrillation risk.
The ProCESS, ARISE, and ProMISe trials showed that protocolized resuscitation with central venous catheter and ScvO2 monitoring did not improve outcomes compared to usual care in septic shock.

The CLASSIC trial found that restrictive fluid therapy after initial resuscitation in septic shock patients in the ICU did not reduce 90-day mortality compared to standard fluid therapy, but reduced fluid volumes overall.

During the COVID-19 pandemic, multiple centers adopted permissive hypoxemia strategies, tolerating SpO2 as low as 88-90% to delay or avoid intubation, given high mortality among mechanically ventilated COVID-19 patients.
The RECOVERY trial showed dexamethasone reduced 28-day mortality in hospitalized COVID-19 patients receiving respiratory support. Standard of care became 6 mg dexamethasone daily for up to 10 days.

High-flow nasal cannula and non-invasive ventilation became preferred initial respiratory support strategies in the COVID-19 era to reduce intubation rates. Awake prone positioning improved oxygenation in non-intubated hypoxemic patients.
source: Ehrmann S et al. Lancet Respir Med 2021

text: ARDSnet protocol: low tidal volume ventilation at 6 mL/kg of predicted body weight with plateau pressure below 30 cmH2O reduces mortality in ARDS. PEEP should be titrated based on FiO2 requirements.

Early COVID-19 era saw substantial empirical antibiotic overuse despite low rates of bacterial co-infection. Stewardship guidance evolved to recommend de-escalation when bacterial infection is unconfirmed.
source: Langford BJ et al. Clin Microbiol Infect 2021

text: Rapid sequence intubation typically uses etomidate or ketamine for induction with rocuronium or succinylcholine for paralysis. Propofol and midazolam infusions maintain post-intubation sedation.
source: Mosier JM et al. Crit Care 2015

text: PROSEVA trial: prone positioning for at least 16 hours daily reduced mortality in severe ARDS with PaO2/FiO2 < 150. Early adoption within 36 hours of ARDS onset is recommended.

Driving pressure (plateau pressure minus PEEP) is a strong predictor of mortality in ARDS. Targeting driving pressure below 15 cmH2O has been associated with improved survival in observational analyses.
The ACURASYS trial showed early neuromuscular blockade with cisatracurium for 48 hours improved survival in severe ARDS (PaO2/FiO2 < 150). The subsequent ROSE trial did not confirm this benefit.
source: Papazian L. NEJM 2010; NHLBI ROSE. NEJM 2019

text: Ventilator-induced lung injury results from excessive tidal volumes (volutrauma), high pressures (barotrauma), repeated opening and closing of alveoli (atelectrauma), and inflammatory mediator release (biotrauma).

Daily spontaneous breathing trials should be conducted in mechanically ventilated patients meeting readiness criteria: FiO2 ≤ 40%, PEEP ≤ 8, adequate cough, and hemodynamic stability without high-dose vasopressors.
Awake prone positioning in non-intubated COVID-19 patients with hypoxemia improved oxygenation and reduced the composite of intubation or death in a meta-analysis of six randomized trials.
source: Ehrmann S et al. Lancet Respir Med 2021

text: Higher PEEP strategies did not consistently reduce mortality in ARDS across meta-analyses but may benefit patients with moderate-severe ARDS. Individualized PEEP titration using esophageal pressure monitoring is an emerging approach.
source: Briel M et al. JAMA 2010; Beitler JR et al. JAMA 2019

text: Early tracheostomy (within 7 days) versus late tracheostomy in mechanically ventilated patients did not reduce 30-day mortality in the TracMan trial but may shorten ICU sedation duration.
Metformin remains the first-line pharmacologic treatment for type 2 diabetes in adults, starting at 500 mg daily and titrating to 2000 mg based on tolerance and HbA1c response.
GLP-1 receptor agonists such as semaglutide and liraglutide reduce HbA1c, promote weight loss, and have cardiovascular benefits in patients with established atherosclerotic disease.
SGLT2 inhibitors including empagliflozin and dapagliflozin reduce cardiovascular mortality and heart failure hospitalization in patients with type 2 diabetes and high cardiovascular risk.
Diabetic ketoacidosis management requires fluid resuscitation with isotonic saline, intravenous insulin infusion at 0.1 U/kg/hr, and potassium replacement when serum potassium falls below 5.3.

Hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state presents with severe hyperglycemia often over 600 mg/dL without significant ketosis. Treatment involves careful fluid replacement and insulin after addressing dehydration.
Annual dilated fundus examination is recommended in type 2 diabetes at diagnosis and in type 1 starting five years after diagnosis. Anti-VEGF agents treat proliferative retinopathy.
Diabetic peripheral neuropathy is managed with glycemic control and symptom relief using duloxetine, pregabalin, or gabapentin. Foot exams detect ulceration risk.
Continuous glucose monitoring improves time-in-range and reduces hypoglycemia in insulin-treated diabetes. Time-in-range targets of 70% between 70-180 mg/dL are recommended.
Type 1 diabetes in children requires multiple daily insulin injections or pump therapy. Carbohydrate counting and insulin-to-carb ratios are taught to families at diagnosis.
Gestational diabetes screening occurs at 24-28 weeks via 50g glucose challenge. Treatment begins with medical nutrition therapy and adds insulin if fasting glucose exceeds 95.
Insulin pump therapy provides continuous subcutaneous insulin infusion and is preferred for type 1 diabetes patients with recurrent hypoglycemia or highly variable glucose levels.
Diabetic foot ulcers require offloading, wound debridement, infection management, and vascular assessment. Neuropathy screening with monofilament is performed annually.
Diabetic nephropathy progression is slowed by ACE inhibitors or ARBs and tight glycemic control. Annual urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio screening is recommended.
Adults with type 2 diabetes should perform at least 150 minutes per week of moderate-intensity aerobic exercise plus resistance training on 2-3 non-consecutive days.
Metabolic surgery should be considered for adults with BMI ≥ 40 or BMI ≥ 35 with inadequately controlled type 2 diabetes despite optimal medical therapy.
Severe hypoglycemia with altered consciousness is treated with intramuscular glucagon 1 mg or intravenous dextrose 25g. Glucose targets should be relaxed in patients with recurrent severe episodes.
Cardiovascular risk reduction in diabetes includes statin therapy, blood pressure control below 130/80, and aspirin for established ASCVD. GLP-1 agonists provide additional CV benefit.
Transition of diabetes care from pediatric to adult services should begin at age 14-16 with structured handoff, self-management education, and dedicated transition clinics.
Automated insulin delivery (artificial pancreas) systems combining CGM with algorithmic pump control significantly improve time-in-range and reduce hypoglycemia in type 1 diabetes.
Women with pre-existing diabetes planning pregnancy should achieve HbA1c < 6.5% before conception. Folic acid supplementation and medication review (discontinuing ACE inhibitors) are essential.
Hip fracture surgery within 48 hours reduces mortality and pressure ulcers in elderly patients. Displaced femoral neck fractures are treated with hemiarthroplasty or total hip arthroplasty.
Bisphosphonates are first-line therapy for postmenopausal osteoporosis. Denosumab or teriparatide are alternatives for high-risk patients with prior fractures.
ACL reconstruction with bone-patellar tendon-bone or hamstring autograft is recommended for young active patients. Physical therapy emphasizes quadriceps strengthening.
Open fractures require antibiotic prophylaxis within 3 hours and surgical irrigation. Gustilo-Anderson grade III fractures need gram-negative coverage added to cefazolin.
Carpal tunnel syndrome is diagnosed clinically with Tinel and Phalen signs, confirmed by nerve conduction studies. Surgical release is indicated for severe or refractory cases.
Acute nonspecific low back pain is managed with activity modification, NSAIDs, and heat therapy. Imaging is reserved for red flags such as trauma or neurologic deficit.
Rotator cuff tears are evaluated with MRI after physical exam. Partial tears often respond to physical therapy; full-thickness tears in active patients benefit from arthroscopic repair.
Knee osteoarthritis management starts with weight loss, physical therapy, and acetaminophen or NSAIDs. Intra-articular corticosteroid injections may provide short-term relief.
Pediatric supracondylar humerus fractures type III require closed reduction and percutaneous pinning. Neurovascular exam before and after reduction is critical.
Acute lateral ankle sprains are treated with RICE protocol, early mobilization, and proprioceptive training. Ottawa ankle rules determine need for radiography.
Degenerative meniscus tears in patients over 40 are initially managed conservatively. Arthroscopic partial meniscectomy shows no benefit over physical therapy in many degenerative cases.

Lumbar spinal stenosis presenting with neurogenic claudication is initially treated conservatively with physical therapy and epidural injections. Surgical decompression is offered for refractory cases.

Periprosthetic joint infection after total joint arthroplasty is diagnosed with elevated CRP, ESR, and synovial fluid analysis. Two-stage exchange arthroplasty remains the gold standard for chronic infections.
First-time anterior shoulder dislocation in patients under 25 has a high recurrence rate. Arthroscopic Bankart repair is offered to young athletes after first dislocation.
VTE prophylaxis after total hip or knee arthroplasty includes LMWH, rivaroxaban, or aspirin for 14-35 days. Mechanical prophylaxis with intermittent pneumatic compression is adjunctive.
Screening mammography in average-risk women is recommended biennially from age 50 to 74. Women with BRCA mutations need earlier screening with MRI adjunct.
Low-dose CT screening for lung cancer is recommended annually in adults 50-80 with 20 pack-year smoking history who are current smokers or quit within 15 years.
Colorectal cancer screening via colonoscopy every 10 years starting at age 45. FIT testing annually is an acceptable alternative.
Febrile neutropenia with ANC below 500 requires empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics within one hour of presentation.
Immune checkpoint inhibitor colitis is managed with high-dose corticosteroids and infliximab for refractory cases. Endoscopy confirms the diagnosis.
Tumor lysis syndrome prophylaxis includes aggressive hydration, allopurinol for low-intermediate risk, and rasburicase for high-risk hematologic malignancies.
CAR-T cell therapy for relapsed B-cell malignancies can cause cytokine release syndrome managed with tocilizumab. ICANS neurotoxicity requires corticosteroid treatment.
Cancer pain management follows the WHO analgesic ladder. Chronic severe pain requires long-acting opioids with breakthrough doses and adjuvants.
PSA screening for prostate cancer is offered to men aged 55-69 after shared decision-making. Overdiagnosis risk must be balanced against early detection benefit.
Cervical cancer screening with HPV testing alone every 5 years is preferred for women 30-65. Pap smear every 3 years is an alternative for ages 21-29.
Borderline resectable pancreatic adenocarcinoma is treated with neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX followed by surgical resection. Adjuvant gemcitabine-capecitabine improves survival after surgery.
Hepatocellular carcinoma in cirrhotic patients is screened with ultrasound and AFP every 6 months. Atezolizumab-bevacizumab is first-line systemic therapy for advanced HCC.
Unresectable melanoma is treated with combination nivolumab-ipilimumab or single-agent anti-PD1. BRAF-mutated melanoma responds to dabrafenib-trametinib targeted therapy.
Glioblastoma standard of care is maximal safe resection followed by concurrent temozolomide and radiation. Tumor treating fields (TTFields) provide additional survival benefit.
Highly emetogenic chemotherapy requires triple antiemetic prophylaxis with NK1 antagonist, 5-HT3 antagonist, and dexamethasone. Olanzapine is added for breakthrough nausea.
Pediatric asthma exacerbations are treated with inhaled albuterol, oral corticosteroids, and oxygen. Admission is indicated for inadequate response after three nebulizer treatments.
Infant bronchiolitis management is supportive with nasal suctioning, hydration, and oxygen. Bronchodilators and corticosteroids are not routinely recommended.
Febrile infants under 29 days require full sepsis workup including blood culture, urine culture, and lumbar puncture with empiric ampicillin and gentamicin.
source: AAP Febrile Infant Clinical Practice Guideline

text: Kawasaki disease is treated with IVIG 2 g/kg and aspirin. Coronary artery aneurysms are monitored with echocardiography.
Moderate to severe croup is treated with nebulized racemic epinephrine and a single oral dose of dexamethasone. Observation for rebound stridor is required.
The CDC childhood immunization schedule includes DTaP, MMR, varicella, hepatitis B, rotavirus, pneumococcal conjugate, and HPV vaccines at age-specific intervals.
Acute otitis media in children over 2 with mild symptoms may use observation for 48-72 hours. Severe cases receive amoxicillin 80-90 mg/kg/day.
ADHD treatment in children 6-11 years combines behavioral therapy with stimulant medication (methylphenidate or amphetamine). Preschool children should start with behavioral therapy alone.
Pediatric obesity is classified by BMI percentile: overweight 85-95th, obese >95th. Treatment includes family-based lifestyle interventions with nutrition counseling and increased physical activity.
Neonatal hyperbilirubinemia is treated with phototherapy when total serum bilirubin exceeds age-specific thresholds. Exchange transfusion is reserved for severe cases unresponsive to phototherapy.
Infantile hypertrophic pyloric stenosis presents with projectile non-bilious vomiting at 3-6 weeks of age. Ultrasound confirms the diagnosis. Ramstedt pyloromyotomy is curative.
Intussusception in children presents with episodic crying, currant jelly stools, and a sausage-shaped mass. Air or hydrostatic enema reduction is first-line treatment.
Simple febrile seizures do not require antiepileptic treatment or neuroimaging. Parental education and fever management are the main interventions.
Nirsevimab, a long-acting monoclonal antibody, is recommended for all infants entering their first RSV season. It replaces monthly palivizumab for most indications.
Failure to thrive is defined as weight below the 2nd percentile or crossing two major percentiles downward. Evaluation includes dietary assessment and screening for organic causes.
Melanoma excision requires margins based on Breslow depth: 0.5 cm for in situ, 1 cm for < 1 mm, 2 cm for 1-2 mm. Sentinel node biopsy indicated for Breslow > 0.8 mm.
Moderate-severe plaque psoriasis responds to biologic agents including IL-17 inhibitors and IL-23 inhibitors when topical therapy fails.
Uncomplicated cellulitis is treated with oral cephalexin. MRSA coverage with doxycycline or TMP-SMX is added for purulent infections.
Stevens-Johnson syndrome requires immediate discontinuation of the offending drug and supportive care in a burn unit for extensive disease.
Atopic dermatitis management includes emollients, topical corticosteroids, and dupilumab for moderate-severe refractory cases.
Moderate acne is treated with topical retinoids plus benzoyl peroxide. Severe nodulocystic acne requires oral isotretinoin with pregnancy prevention counseling.
Chronic spontaneous urticaria is treated with second-generation antihistamines at up to four times the standard dose. Omalizumab is added for refractory cases.
Basal cell carcinoma is most commonly treated with Mohs micrographic surgery for high-risk facial lesions. Standard excision with 4mm margins is adequate for low-risk tumors.
Tinea corporis is treated with topical antifungals for 2-4 weeks. Tinea capitis requires systemic therapy with terbinafine or griseofulvin for 6-8 weeks.
Vitiligo is treated with topical calcineurin inhibitors, narrow-band UVB phototherapy, and the recently approved topical ruxolitinib JAK inhibitor.
Acute ischemic stroke within 4.5 hours is treated with tPA 0.9 mg/kg. Mechanical thrombectomy is indicated for large vessel occlusion up to 24 hours.
Status epilepticus is initially treated with IV lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg followed by levetiracetam or fosphenytoin for refractory seizures.
Migraine prophylaxis with CGRP antagonists such as erenumab reduces attack frequency in chronic migraine.
Parkinson disease is treated with levodopa-carbidopa as the most effective symptomatic therapy. MAO-B inhibitors are adjunctive options.
Relapsing-remitting MS is treated with disease-modifying therapies including ocrelizumab or natalizumab to reduce relapses.
Severe TBI management includes intracranial pressure monitoring with EVD, hyperosmolar therapy with mannitol or hypertonic saline, and avoidance of hypotension.
Anti-amyloid antibodies lecanemab and donanemab showed modest slowing of cognitive decline in early Alzheimer disease. ARIA monitoring with MRI is required.
Neuropathic pain is first-line treated with gabapentinoids, SNRIs, or tricyclic antidepressants. Topical lidocaine and capsaicin are adjuncts.
Guillain-Barré syndrome is treated with IVIG or plasma exchange. Forced vital capacity monitoring guides intubation decisions when FVC falls below 20 mL/kg.
Myasthenia gravis is treated with acetylcholinesterase inhibitors and immunosuppression. Thymectomy benefits patients with thymoma or generalized disease.
AF with CHA2DS2-VASc ≥ 2 requires anticoagulation with DOACs such as apixaban or rivaroxaban. Warfarin is an alternative.
HFrEF benefits from guideline-directed therapy including ACEi/ARNI, beta-blocker, MRA, and SGLT2 inhibitor.
Stable angina management includes beta-blockers or CCBs with antiplatelet therapy and statins.
Hypertension stage 1 with elevated CV risk warrants pharmacologic therapy. Goal BP is below 130/80 in most adults.
High-intensity statin therapy is recommended for secondary prevention after atherosclerotic cardiovascular events.
Severe symptomatic aortic stenosis in patients at high surgical risk is treated with transcatheter aortic valve replacement (TAVR) with improved outcomes compared to medical therapy.
STEMI requires emergent PCI within 90 minutes of first medical contact. Dual antiplatelet with aspirin and P2Y12 inhibitor and anticoagulation are standard.
NSTEMI risk stratification with TIMI or GRACE score guides invasive versus conservative strategy. High-risk patients benefit from early invasive approach within 24 hours.
Infective endocarditis requires 4-6 weeks of IV antibiotics. Surgical intervention is indicated for heart failure, uncontrolled infection, or vegetation > 10mm with emboli.
Acute pericarditis is treated with colchicine plus NSAIDs. Cardiac tamponade requires emergent pericardiocentesis.
Acute pulmonary embolism with hemodynamic instability requires systemic thrombolysis with alteplase. Submassive PE management includes anticoagulation and risk stratification with RV function.
Syncope evaluation begins with history, ECG, and orthostatic vitals. High-risk features include syncope during exertion, abnormal ECG, or family history of sudden death.
HFpEF management now includes SGLT2 inhibitors after the EMPEROR-Preserved and DELIVER trials showed reduced heart failure hospitalization.
Primary prevention ICD implantation is indicated for LVEF ≤ 35% despite optimal medical therapy for at least 3 months in patients with NYHA class II-III heart failure.
Peripheral arterial disease is screened with ankle-brachial index. Claudication is managed with supervised exercise and cilostazol. Revascularization is reserved for critical limb ischemia.
GERD is managed with proton pump inhibitors. Lifestyle modifications include weight loss, head-of-bed elevation, and avoidance of late meals.
Moderate-severe Crohn disease is treated with anti-TNF agents including infliximab or adalimumab, with ustekinumab as alternative.
UC flares are treated with mesalamine for mild disease and corticosteroids followed by biologics for moderate-severe cases.
Cirrhosis with varices requires nonselective beta-blockers for primary prophylaxis. Active variceal hemorrhage is managed with endoscopic band ligation and octreotide.
Acute pancreatitis management includes aggressive fluid resuscitation with lactated Ringer, pain control, and early enteral nutrition.
First episode of non-severe C. difficile is treated with fidaxomicin. Vancomycin is an alternative. Fecal microbiota transplant is reserved for recurrent episodes.
Celiac disease is diagnosed with tissue transglutaminase IgA antibody and confirmed by duodenal biopsy. Lifelong gluten-free diet is the only treatment.
Upper GI bleeding management includes IV PPI infusion, blood transfusion with threshold hemoglobin of 7 g/dL, and endoscopy within 24 hours.
IBS-D is treated with low FODMAP diet, loperamide, and rifaximin. IBS-C responds to linaclotide or lubiprostone. CBT improves outcomes across subtypes.

NAFLD management focuses on weight loss of 7-10% body weight through lifestyle modification. Pioglitazone or vitamin E may benefit biopsy-proven NASH. Resmetirom (thyroid hormone receptor agonist) was recently approved.
Hypothyroidism is treated with levothyroxine 1.6 mcg/kg daily, with TSH rechecked at 6-8 weeks.
Adrenal crisis requires immediate hydrocortisone 100 mg IV and saline resuscitation.
Thyroid storm requires propylthiouracil, beta-blockade, iodine after antithyroid therapy, and corticosteroids.
Pheochromocytoma is managed with alpha-blockade (phenoxybenzamine) before surgery, followed by beta-blockade. Surgical resection is curative.
Cushing syndrome diagnosis uses 24-hour urinary free cortisol, late-night salivary cortisol, and low-dose dexamethasone suppression test. Transsphenoidal surgery is first-line for pituitary adenoma.
MDD is treated with SSRIs as first-line agents, combined with CBT for moderate-severe cases.
GAD responds to SSRIs or SNRIs with CBT. Benzodiazepines are reserved for short-term use.
First-episode schizophrenia is treated with second-generation antipsychotics. Long-acting injectables improve adherence.
Bipolar I disorder manic episodes are treated with lithium, valproate, or atypical antipsychotics. Maintenance therapy prevents both manic and depressive episodes.
PTSD is treated with trauma-focused CBT, CPT, or EMDR as first-line. SSRIs (sertraline, paroxetine) are first-line pharmacotherapy.
Acute DVT is treated with DOACs for at least 3 months, with extended duration for unprovoked events.
CKD management includes ACEi/ARB for proteinuria and SGLT2 inhibitor for diabetic CKD.
Prerenal AKI from volume depletion responds to crystalloid resuscitation. Avoid nephrotoxins.
Anaphylaxis requires immediate intramuscular epinephrine 0.3 mg. Adjunctive therapy includes antihistamines and corticosteroids.
Uncomplicated cystitis is treated with nitrofurantoin 100 mg BID for 5 days or TMP-SMX for 3 days.
Outpatient CAP is treated with amoxicillin or doxycycline in healthy adults. Respiratory fluoroquinolones are alternatives.
Rheumatoid arthritis is treated with methotrexate as first-line DMARD. Biologics (TNF inhibitors, IL-6 inhibitors) are added for inadequate response.
Acute gout flares are treated with colchicine, NSAIDs, or corticosteroids. Urate-lowering therapy with allopurinol targets serum urate below 6 mg/dL.
SLE management includes hydroxychloroquine for all patients, with immunosuppressives (mycophenolate, cyclophosphamide) for severe organ involvement.
HIV treatment with integrase inhibitor-based regimens (bictegravir/FTC/TAF) is recommended for treatment-naive patients. PrEP with emtricitabine/tenofovir prevents transmission.
